On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He hired craftsmen from [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the [[:en:Toyotomi clan|Toyotomi family.]] The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q874428]] Wikidata name: Ōsaki Hachimangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōsaki Hachimangū
大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He hired craftsmen from [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the [[:en:Toyotomi clan|Toyotomi family.]] The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Osaki-Hachiman-gu}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/ Ōsaki Hachimangū official site] {{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf Ōsaki Hachimangū English pamphlet] {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ Hachimangū]][[sn:Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}} [[sn:Ōsaki Hachimangū]] [[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]] [[sn:saki Hachimangū]] [[sn: failed]] [[ja:大崎八幡宮]] [[Category:Date family of Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings in {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q874428]] Wikidata name: Ōsaki Hachimangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōsaki Hachimangū
大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He hired craftsmen from [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the [[:en:Toyotomi clan|Toyotomi family.]] The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Osaki-Hachiman-gu}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/ Ōsaki Hachimangū official site] {{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf Ōsaki Hachimangū English pamphlet] {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ Hachimangū]][[sn:Ōsaki Hachimangū]]}} [[sn:Ōsaki Hachimangū]] [[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]] [[sn:saki Hachimangū]] [[sn: failed]] [[ja:大崎八幡宮]] [[Category:Date family of Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings in Property of Japan in Miyagi prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Miyagi prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Tangible Cultural Properties in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]] [[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]] [[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]] [[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]] [[ko:오사키하치만궁]] [[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]] [[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]] [[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]] [[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]] [[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]] [[ko:오사키하치만궁]] [[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]] [[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q874428}}

大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He hired craftsmen from [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the [[:en:Toyotomi clan|Toyotomi family.]] The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Osaki-Hachiman-gu}} * The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He hired craftsmen from [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the [[:en:Toyotomi clan|Toyotomi family.]] The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). 大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He hired craftsmen from [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the [[:en:Toyotomi clan|Toyotomi family.]] The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Osaki-Hachiman-gu}} * [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[sn:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q874428]] Wikidata name: Ōsaki Hachimangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōsaki Hachimangū
大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He hired craftsmen from [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the [[:en:Toyotomi clan|Toyotomi family.]] The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[sn:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q874428]] Wikidata name: Ōsaki Hachimangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōsaki Hachimangū
大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He hired craftsmen from [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the [[:en:Toyotomi clan|Toyotomi family.]] The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Osaki-Hachiman-gu}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/ Ōsaki Hachimangū official site] {{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf Ōsaki Hachimangū English pamphlet] {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]] [[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]] [[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]] [[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]] [[ko:오사키하치만궁]] [[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]] [[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q874428}} [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

revision.text:
[[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[sn:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q874428]] Wikidata name: Ōsaki Hachimangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōsaki Hachimangū
大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba-ku]], [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. He hired craftsmen from [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the [[:en:Toyotomi clan|Toyotomi family.]] The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the [[:en:Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni shrine]] in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Osaki-Hachiman-gu}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/ Ōsaki Hachimangū official site] {{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf Ōsaki Hachimangū English pamphlet] {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama]] architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 [[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]] (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular [[:en:Dormer|Dormer]] with a decorative [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Osaki-Hachiman-gu}} * [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[co:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[co:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[sn:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q874428]] Wikidata name: Ōsaki Hachimangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōsaki Hachimangū
大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|en|Aoba-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1042308|lt=Aoba-ku}}, {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi}} | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|en|Aoba-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1042308|lt=Aoba-ku}}, {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan}}. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' {{ill|Date Masamune|en|Date Masamune|WD=Q311183|lt=Date Masamune}}. He hired craftsmen from {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the {{ill|Toyotomi clan|en|Toyotomi clan|WD=Q1521658|lt=Toyotomi family.}} The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the {{ill|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|WD=Q709822|lt=Toyokuni shrine}} in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama}} architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}} (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular {{ill|Dormer|en|Dormer|WD=Q17345|lt=Dormer}} with a decorative {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard}} of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''{{ill|Karahafu|en|Karahafu|WD=Q977293|lt=Karahafu}}'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|WD=Q3245520|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)}} ==References== [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[co:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[co:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[sn:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q874428]] Wikidata name: Ōsaki Hachimangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōsaki Hachimangū
大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|en|Aoba-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1042308|lt=Aoba-ku}}, {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi}} | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|en|Aoba-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1042308|lt=Aoba-ku}}, {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan}}. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' {{ill|Date Masamune|en|Date Masamune|WD=Q311183|lt=Date Masamune}}. He hired craftsmen from {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the {{ill|Toyotomi clan|en|Toyotomi clan|WD=Q1521658|lt=Toyotomi family.}} The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the {{ill|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|WD=Q709822|lt=Toyokuni shrine}} in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama}} architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}} (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular {{ill|Dormer|en|Dormer|WD=Q17345|lt=Dormer}} with a decorative {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard}} of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''{{ill|Karahafu|en|Karahafu|WD=Q977293|lt=Karahafu}}'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|WD=Q3245520|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Osaki-Hachiman-gu}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/ Ōsaki Hachimangū official site] {{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf Ōsaki Hachimangū English pamphlet] {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|大崎八幡宮|version=103848578|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōsaki Hachimangū]] Hachiman-gū]] [[ko:오사키하치만궁]] [[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]] [[zh:大崎八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q874428}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}} (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular {{ill|Dormer|en|Dormer|WD=Q17345|lt=Dormer}} with a decorative {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard}} of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''{{ill|Karahafu|en|Karahafu|WD=Q977293|lt=Karahafu}}'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|WD=Q3245520|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)}} ==References== [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[co:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[co:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] [[arz:اوساكى هاشتشمنجى]][[sn:commons:Category:Osaki-Hachiman-gu]][[en:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[es:Ōsaki Hachimangū]][[eu:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Ōsaki Hachiman-gū]][[ja:大崎八幡宮]][[ko:오사키하치만궁]][[ru:Храм Осаки Хатимангу]][[zh:大崎八幡宮]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōsaki Hachimangū
大崎八幡宮 | image = File:Osaki Hachiman-gu 20130819b.jpg | caption = ''Haiden'' of Ōsaki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.2722|N|140.845|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 4-6-1 Hachiman, {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|en|Aoba-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1042308|lt=Aoba-ku|1=Aoba-ku, Sendai}}, {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai|1=Sendai}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} | website = {{URL|http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Ōsaki Hachimangū'''|大崎八幡宮}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|en|Aoba-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1042308|lt=Aoba-ku|1=Aoba-ku, Sendai}}, {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai|1=Sendai}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The {{nihongo|main shrine building|社殿|shaden}} has been designated a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. ==History== The construction of the present ''shaden'' was ordered by the wealthy ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' {{ill|Date Masamune|en|Date Masamune|WD=Q311183|lt=Date Masamune|1=Date Masamune}}. He hired craftsmen from {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and central Japan, some of whom had previously worked for the {{ill|Toyotomi clan|en|Toyotomi clan|WD=Q1521658|lt=Toyotomi family.|1=Toyotomi clan}} The shrine was erected between 1604 and 1607. The lavish decoration with wood carving, painting, metal fittings, and lacquer emulated recent models from central Japan, in particular, the {{ill|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|WD=Q709822|lt=Toyokuni shrine|1=Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)}} in Kyoto.{{Cite book|title=Osaki Hachiman: Architecture, Materiality and Samurai Power in Seventeenth-Century Japan|last=Schweizer|first=Anton|publisher=Dietrich Reimer Verlag GmbH|year=2016|isbn=978-3496015413|location=Berlin|pages=206–228}} Since the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} the shrine was called Ōsaki Hachiman Jinja. In consideration of historical circumstances, its original name was reinstated in June 1997. On November 22, 1952, the ''shaden'' was designated as a National Treasure. ==Architecture== The ''shaden'' is one of the oldest extant examples of {{nihongo|''[[ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''|石の間造}}, also known as ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]'' (権現造), and an outstanding work of {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}} architecture. It is a single-storied structure consisting of a main sanctuary (''[[honden]]'') and a worship hall (''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'') which are joined via a connecting passage called ''ishi-no-ma''. All three structures are under a single roof which is covered with thin [[:en:Shake (shingle)|shake shingles]] (''kokerabuki'' 柿葺) made from Japanese cypress.{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine – English information | publisher = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine | access-date = 2009-11-04 }}{{Cite web | publisher = [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | trans-title = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = ja | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }} The ''honden'' is a 5 {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}} (間, bay) by 3 ken structure with a hip-and-gable, [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] style roof to which a simple [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]] of the 3 ken by 1 ken ''ishi-no-ma'' connects. The ''haiden'' is also 3 ken deep and 7 ken wide. Its roof is like that of the ''honden'' of the hip-and-gable type. On the front it has an attached triangular {{ill|Dormer|en|Dormer|WD=Q17345|lt=Dormer|1=Dormer}} with a decorative {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard|1=Bargeboard}} of strongly concave shape, a {{nihongo|''chidori hafu''|千鳥破風|lit. "plover gable"}}.{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chidorihafu.htm | title = chidori hafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} The entrance is covered by a 5 ken wide step canopy with an undulating ''{{ill|Karahafu|en|Karahafu|WD=Q977293|lt=Karahafu|1=Karahafu}}'' gable at eave ends{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | title = karahafu | publisher = [[JAANUS|JAANUS – Japanese Architecture and Art Net User System]] | access-date = 2009-11-08 }} (''nokikarahafu''). ==See also== *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|WD=Q3245520|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (shrines)|1=List of National
